era_context: The year 1190 falls in the early stages of the Third Crusade (1189–1192), a major military campaign to recapture Jerusalem from Saladin. In Europe, Richard I was preparing for the Crusade; the Angevin Empire was under pressure from Philip II of France. In the Islamic world, Saladin was consolidating his control over the Levant following his capture of Jerusalem in 1187. In South Asia, the Delhi region was experiencing the early stages of Islamic expansion. In East Asia, the Kamakura Shogunate was consolidating power in Japan; the Jin Dynasty controlled northern China. The Khmer Empire under Jayavarman VII was at its height.

events.title: Third Crusade: Departure of Crusader Forces
events.description: In 1190, major Crusader forces departed Europe for the Levant to participate in the Third Crusade. Richard I of England and Philip II of France were preparing their forces for the campaign. The German Crusaders, led by Emperor Frederick Barbarossa, departed in 1190 and traveled overland through the Balkans and Anatolia. Frederick Barbarossa died during the journey (in June 1190), but his forces continued to the Levant. The departure of these major Crusader forces marked the beginning of the Third Crusade's main campaign. The Crusade was motivated by Saladin's capture of Jerusalem in 1187 and represented a major military effort by Western European powers to recapture the Holy Land.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Third Crusade

events.certainty_note: The departure of Crusader forces in 1190 is well-documented in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The dates and circumstances are securely established.

events.title: Death of Frederick Barbarossa
events.description: On 10 June 1190, Frederick I Barbarossa, Holy Roman Emperor, died while leading the German Crusaders to the Levant. Frederick had been one of the most powerful rulers in Europe and had been leading a large German Crusader force. His death occurred in Cilicia (in modern-day Turkey) while the Crusaders were traveling through Anatolia. Frederick's death was a significant blow to the Third Crusade, as he was one of the most powerful Crusader leaders. However, his death also had significant consequences for the Holy Roman Empire: it marked the end of the Staufen dynasty's most powerful period and contributed to the fragmentation of imperial authority. Frederick's death also affected the succession of the German throne, leading to a contested election.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Frederick Barbarossa's death

events.certainty_note: Frederick Barbarossa's death on 10 June 1190 is well-documented in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date and circumstances are securely established.

events.title: Saladin's Consolidation of Power in the Levant
events.description: In 1190, Saladin continued to consolidate his control over the Levant and Egypt following his capture of Jerusalem in 1187. He worked to establish administrative structures in the territories he had conquered and to prepare for the anticipated Crusader response to his conquest of Jerusalem. Saladin was aware that the Third Crusade was being organized and was taking steps to strengthen his military and administrative capacity. The year 1190 thus represents a period of consolidation and preparation for Saladin, as he worked to secure his gains and prepare for the coming conflict with the Crusaders.
events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of power in 1190 is well-documented in historiographic sources, but specific events of this year are not extensively recorded in contemporary sources.

events.title: Minamoto Yoritomo's Consolidation of Power in Japan
events.description: In 1190, Minamoto Yoritomo continued to consolidate his military and political authority over Japan. Having defeated the Taira clan in 1185, Yoritomo was working to establish the institutional structures of military rule that would eventually become the Kamakura Shogunate. In 1190, Yoritomo was at the height of his power and was beginning to formalize the administrative structures that would govern Japan. The year 1190 falls in the period between Yoritomo's military victory over the Taira and his formal appointment as Shogun in 1192.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Kamakura Shogunate origins

events.certainty_note: Yoritomo's consolidation of power is documented in Japanese sources, but the precise chronology of events in 1190 is less certain.

events.title: Jayavarman VII's Continued Expansion of the Khmer Empire
events.description: In 1190, Jayavarman VII continued his consolidation of power over the Khmer Empire. He had recently defeated the Chams (who had invaded Khmer territory) and was expanding Khmer territory. Jayavarman VII was undertaking massive construction projects, including the expansion of Angkor Thom and the construction of the Bayon temple complex. The year 1190 falls in the middle of his reign, a period of relative stability and prosperity. Jayavarman VII's reign represented the peak of Khmer power and cultural achievement.
events.certainty_note: Jayavarman VII's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented through archaeological evidence and inscriptions. The precise chronology of events in 1190 is less certain.

events.title: Henry VI's Consolidation of the Holy Roman Empire
events.description: In 1190, Henry VI succeeded his father Frederick Barbarossa as Holy Roman Emperor. Henry VI was working to consolidate his authority over the fragmented territories of the Holy Roman Empire and his Sicilian inheritance. The year 1190 marked the beginning of Henry VI's reign, which would be marked by attempts to strengthen imperial authority and to expand Staufen power. However, Henry VI's reign was also marked by tensions between imperial authority and the autonomy of German princes, a conflict that would define the Empire for centuries.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Henry VI's reign

events.certainty_note: Henry VI's succession to the imperial throne in 1190 is well-documented in contemporary sources. The date and circumstances are securely established.

events.title: Philip II's Expansion in France
events.description: In 1190, Philip II of France continued his strategy of expanding Capetian territory at the expense of the Angevin domains. With Richard I preparing for the Crusade, Philip exploited the opportunity to launch military campaigns in Normandy and Anjou. These campaigns were part of a broader strategy to dismantle the Angevin Empire and consolidate Capetian power in France. The territorial gains of 1190 and the surrounding years would have lasting consequences for the balance of power in France and would contribute to the eventual collapse of the Angevin Empire in the early 13th century.
key_figures: Richard I (King of England, preparing for Crusade)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Philip II's expansion

events.certainty_note: Philip II's general strategy of expansion is well-documented, but the specific campaigns and dates of 1190 are less precisely recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Frederick Barbarossa's death in 1190 is sometimes overstated as marking an immediate collapse of German power. While his death was significant, the German Crusaders continued to the Levant and participated in the Third Crusade, albeit with reduced effectiveness. The notion that Frederick's death 'crippled' the Third Crusade is complicated by the fact that the Crusade achieved significant military successes in 1191–1192, even without Frederick's leadership. Additionally, Henry VI's succession to the imperial throne was not immediately contested, and he was able to consolidate his authority relatively quickly, suggesting that the transition was smoother than sometimes portrayed.
historiographic_note: The year 1190 is well-documented for major political and military events in Europe and the Islamic world, particularly regarding the Third Crusade and Frederick Barbarossa's death. European sources provide rich documentation of the Crusade's preparation and the German Crusaders' departure. Islamic sources document Saladin's consolidation of power. East Asian sources document Yoritomo's consolidation of power and Jayavarman VII's reign. However, documentation is heavily skewed toward elite political and military history. Economic and social developments are poorly recorded for most regions. African, Oceanic, and much of Central Asian history is almost entirely absent from contemporary documentation. The sources also reflect significant biases: European sources are dominated by Crusade narratives; Islamic sources emphasize Saladin's leadership and the defense of Islamic territories; East Asian sources focus on court and administrative history.

graph_edges.note: The departure of the German Crusaders led to Frederick Barbarossa's death during the journey
Frederick Barbarossa's death led to Henry VI's succession as Holy Roman Emperor
The departure of Crusader forces in 1190 led to their arrival in the Levant in 1191
Saladin's consolidation of power in 1190 set the stage for his conflict with the Crusaders in 1191
